The blurred line between products and services, which puts consumers at a disadvantage and potentially favours fraud, should be clarified and clarified by the Digital Fairness Act.
not only in small print by an EULA or general terms and conditions
clear obligation to do so before a purchase is concluded

paragraphs.text: My proposal is to include digital rights management and the digital market in the Digital Fairness Act to protect consumers of digital goods from over-privileging. Products such as audio books, music, films and video games, which are often purchased and collected in digital form today, should be more clearly regulated. Digital products should also be covered by the same regulations as physical products or, in the case of a simple digital licence, a clear obligation to do so before a purchase is concluded, and not only in small print by an EULA or general terms and conditions, which can very often only be viewed after the purchase has been concluded. In general, I would like to see the digital market and its fairness play a role in the Digital Fairness Act and be fairer for consumers in the future. In my view, ownership and ownership of digital purchases need regulation to address platform constraints, allways online and the loss of digital content as a result of the seller’s deletion. The blurred line between products and services, which puts consumers at a disadvantage and potentially favours fraud, should be clarified and clarified by the Digital Fairness Act.
